title: Causal Language Modeling Loss
concept: The model minimizes cross-entropy loss on every target token in parallel during pretraining, using causal masking to block future tokens.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of a Causal Language Model during pretraining?

options.text: Predict the next token in a sequence

exercises.explain: Causal Language Models are trained to predict the next token in a sequence based on the tokens that came before it.

cards.title: Learning Word by Word
cards.body: When we train an AI to write stories, how does it learn from its mistakes? It doesn't just get a grade at the end of the book. It gets graded on every single word it tries to guess next!

cards.title: What is Causal Loss?
cards.body: Causal Language Modeling Loss measures how bad the model is at predicting the next word. It calculates the 'cross-entropy loss' for every single token in the input text at the same time, using a causal mask to make sure the model only uses past words to predict the future.

cards.title: The Sliding Card Game
cards.body: Imagine teaching a kid to read a sentence aloud, one word at a time, hiding the rest of the sentence with a sliding card. Every time they slide the card to reveal the next word, you check if they guessed it right and correct them. The model does this for millions of words at once, learning from its mistakes at each step.

cards.title: Predicting 'Cats like milk'
cards.body:
If the input is "Cats like milk", the model tries to predict:
1. "like" after seeing "Cats"
2. "milk" after seeing "Cats like"

The loss measures how low the model's confidence was for the correct words ("like" and "milk") and updates the model to do better next time.

cards.title: Myth: Does it train one word at a time?
cards.body: No! Thanks to 'causal masking', the model processes the entire sentence at once, but digitally blocks itself from 'looking ahead'. This makes training incredibly fast because it calculates the loss for all words in parallel.

cards.title: Comparing Attention Limits
cards.body: Standard models look at everything at once, but causal models must restrict their view to learn step-by-step prediction.

left.text: Can see both past and future words at the same time. Used for understanding text.

right.text: Can only see past words. Future words are hidden. Used for generating text.

exercises.prompt: Which loss function is commonly used to train Causal Language Models?
exercises.explain: Cross-entropy loss measures the difference between the model's predicted probability distribution and the actual target word. Lower loss means better predictions.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Causal masking is used to hide future tokens from the model during training.
exercises.explain: Causal masking ensures the model cannot look at future tokens when predicting the current token, preventing it from 'cheating' during training.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how causal masking protects the training process.
exercises.sentence: Causal masking prevents the model from cheating by blocking access to _____ tokens.
exercises.explain: Causal masking blocks the model from seeing future tokens, ensuring it only learns to predict what comes next using past information.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI that correctly describes how it calculates Causal Language Modeling Loss.

options.text: I hide random words in the middle of a sentence and train the model to fill in the blank spaces.
I feed the model a sequence and calculate loss on its ability to predict the next token at each step, masking future tokens.
I grade the model based on whether the final sentence is grammatically correct according to a set of rules.

exercises.explain: AI A is incorrect because it describes masked language modeling (like BERT), not causal language modeling. AI B correctly describes predicting the next token using causal masking.

exercises.prompt: How does a Causal Language Model calculate its training loss across a sentence?

options.text: It calculates cross-entropy loss for every token position in parallel, masking out future tokens.
It hides random words in the middle of a sentence and asks the model to fill them in.
It compares the entire generated paragraph to a human score at the very end of the text.
It only calculates loss on the very last word of a sentence to save computer memory.

exercises.explain: Causal Language Modeling trains the model by predicting the very next token at every position in the sequence simultaneously, using a causal mask to prevent it from looking ahead.
